title: Branching and Merging
learningObjectives: Understand branches as lightweight pointers to commits
Create, switch between, and delete branches
Understand what HEAD is and its role in navigation

theory.content:
What is a Branch?

A branch in Git is not a copy of your code — it is simply a lightweight, movable pointer to a commit. Internally, a branch is stored as a 41-byte file (a SHA-1 hash plus a newline) in .git/refs/heads/. Creating a branch is an O(1) operation regardless of how large your repository is — there is no file copying involved.

HEAD is a special pointer that tells Git which branch you are currently on. It is stored in .git/HEAD and normally contains a symbolic reference like ref: refs/heads/main. When you make a new commit, the current branch pointer advances to the new commit, and HEAD follows. In detached HEAD state, HEAD points directly to a commit SHA rather than a branch name — this happens when you check out a specific tag or commit hash.

Creating and Switching Branches

Git 2.23 introduced git switch as a focused replacement for the branch-switching part of git checkout. It is now the preferred command:

# Create a new branch (does not switch to it)
git branch feature-x

# Switch to an existing branch
git switch feature-x

# Create AND switch in one step (preferred)
git switch -c feature-x

# Legacy equivalent (still valid)
git checkout -b feature-x

# List all local branches
git branch

# List with last commit info
git branch -v

The -c flag in git switch -c stands for "create". Use it whenever you start new work to keep your main branch clean.

Fast-Forward vs 3-Way Merge

When you run git merge feature-x from the main branch, Git chooses the merge strategy based on the history:

Fast-forward merge: If the target branch (main) has not received any new commits since the feature branch was created, Git simply moves the main pointer forward to point at the feature branch's tip. No merge commit is created. The history stays linear.

3-way merge: If both branches have diverged (each has new commits the other lacks), Git finds the common ancestor commit and combines the changes from both branches. This creates a new merge commit with two parents.

# Perform a merge (auto-selects strategy)
git merge feature-x

# Force a merge commit even when fast-forward is possible
git merge --no-ff feature-x

# Preview what would be merged (dry run)
git merge --no-commit --no-ff feature-x

The --no-ff flag is useful in team workflows where you want merge commits to explicitly record that a feature branch was integrated, preserving that context in the log.

Merge Conflicts

A conflict occurs when the same lines in the same file were modified differently on both branches. Git cannot automatically decide which version to keep, so it pauses the merge and marks the conflict in the file:

<<<<<<< HEAD
This is the version from the current branch (main)
=======
This is the version from the incoming branch (feature-x)
>>>>>>> feature-x

The section between <<<<<<< and ======= is your current branch's content. The section between ======= and >>>>>>> is the incoming branch's content. To resolve the conflict:

  1. Edit the file and remove the conflict markers, keeping the desired content
  2. Stage the resolved file: git add <file>
  3. Complete the merge: git commit

For complex conflicts, use a visual merge tool: git mergetool — this opens your configured diff tool (e.g., vimdiff, VS Code, IntelliJ).

Deleting Branches

After a feature branch has been merged, it is good practice to delete it to keep the branch list manageable:

# Safe delete — only succeeds if the branch is fully merged
git branch -d feature-x

# Force delete — removes even if unmerged (data may be lost!)
git branch -D feature-x

# List all branches, including remote-tracking branches
git branch -a

# Show each branch with its latest commit
git branch -v

Note: you cannot delete the branch you are currently on. Switch to another branch first.
